Core Mechanism & Structure: DLinear decomposes the input time series into seasonal and trend components using a moving average filter. It then independently applies two separate linear layers—one for the seasonal component and one for the trend component—to project the historical observations to the future prediction length. Finally, the forecasted seasonal and trend components are summed to produce the final prediction. It offers an 'individual' mode where linear layers are applied per variate or a shared mode across all variates.
Core Innovations: Decomposition-based Linear Modeling: Demonstrates that complex non-linear models or attention mechanisms might be unnecessary when a series is properly decomposed, achieving competitive results with simple linear layers.
Explicit Series Decomposition: Leverages moving average to separate time series into seasonal and trend components, which are then modeled independently.

Individual vs. Shared Linear Projections: Provides flexibility to apply linear transformations either uniquely for each time series variate or as a shared transformation across all variates, catering to different multivariate data characteristics.

Key Technical Details.Input Embedding: No explicit input embedding layer; raw time series values are directly processed after potential normalization.
Key Technical Details.Feature Extraction: Feature extraction is primarily achieved through series decomposition, followed by simple linear transformations (fully connected layers) applied to the decomposed components. No complex convolutional or attention-based feature extractors are used in the core model.
Key Technical Details.Positional Encoding: No explicit positional encoding mechanism is used. Temporal relationships are implicitly learned by the linear layers mapping the input sequence length to the prediction length.
Key Technical Details.Non-stationarity Handling: Handles non-stationarity primarily through series decomposition (`series_decomp`), which separates the time series into a stationary (residual) seasonal component and a non-stationary trend component.

Direct Observations.claim: Uses series decomposition via moving average
Direct Observations.evidence: In `models\DLinear.py`, `self.decompsition = series_decomp(configs.moving_avg)` is instantiated. The `series_decomp` class is defined in `layers\Autoformer_EncDec.py`, using `moving_avg`.

Direct Observations.claim: Employs separate linear layers for seasonal and trend components
Direct Observations.evidence: In `models\DLinear.py`, `self.Linear_Seasonal` and `self.Linear_Trend` (either `nn.Linear` or `nn.ModuleList[nn.Linear]`) are defined and applied in the `encoder` method.

Direct Observations.claim: Supports multiple tasks (forecasting, imputation, anomaly detection, classification)
Direct Observations.evidence: The `forward` method in `models\DLinear.py` conditionally calls `forecast`, `imputation`, `anomaly_detection`, or `classification` based on `self.task_name`.

Direct Observations.claim: Provides an option for individual linear models per variate
Direct Observations.evidence: The `individual` boolean flag in `__init__` of `models\DLinear.py` determines whether `self.Linear_Seasonal` and `self.Linear_Trend` are `nn.ModuleList` (individual) or single `nn.Linear` layers (shared).

Direct Observations.claim: Does not use explicit attention mechanisms
Direct Observations.evidence: The `Model` class in `models\DLinear.py` does not import or instantiate any attention-related classes (e.g., from `layers\Autoformer_EncDec.py` such as `EncoderLayer` or `DecoderLayer` which contain attention). Only `series_decomp` is imported.

Direct Observations.claim: Does not use explicit positional encoding
Direct Observations.evidence: No `PositionalEmbedding` or similar structures are found or instantiated in `models\DLinear.py`.

Strengths.label: Computational Efficiency
Strengths.why: The model relies on moving average filters and simple linear transformations, which have significantly lower computational complexity (O(L*C) to O(L_in * L_out * C)) compared to attention-based or complex recurrent models.
Strengths.conditions: Especially advantageous for long sequences or large numbers of variates where quadratic complexity is prohibitive.

Strengths.label: Simplicity and Interpretability
Strengths.why: Linear models are generally more interpretable than deep neural networks, and the explicit decomposition into seasonal and trend components offers a clear understanding of the model's operation.
Strengths.conditions: When model explainability is a key requirement.

Strengths.label: Effective for Stationary/Periodic Data
Strengths.why: The decomposition method effectively separates trend, making the residual seasonal component more stationary and predictable with linear models. This works well for data with strong seasonality.
Strengths.conditions: When time series exhibit clear trends and periodic patterns.

Strengths.label: Adaptability to Various Tasks
Strengths.why: The underlying encoder (decomposition + linear projection) is versatile enough to be reused for forecasting, imputation, anomaly detection, and classification with minimal changes.
Strengths.conditions: When a single architectural backbone is preferred for multiple time series tasks.

Weaknesses.label: Limited Capacity for Complex Non-linear Dynamics
Weaknesses.why: Being a purely linear model (after decomposition), it may struggle to capture highly complex, chaotic, or non-linear relationships and interactions within the time series.
Weaknesses.conditions: When the underlying data generation process is highly non-linear or exhibits intricate dependencies.

Weaknesses.label: Sensitivity to Decomposition Quality
Weaknesses.why: The model's performance heavily relies on the effectiveness of the moving average decomposition. If seasonality is irregular, multiple seasonalities exist, or the trend is complex, the decomposition might be suboptimal.
Weaknesses.conditions: For time series with multiple seasonal patterns or highly volatile/irregular seasonality.

Weaknesses.label: No Explicit Cross-Series Interactions (in individual mode)
Weaknesses.why: In `individual=True` mode, each variate is modeled completely independently, ignoring potential cross-variate correlations that could be crucial for multivariate forecasting.
Weaknesses.conditions: When strong and complex inter-dependencies exist between different time series variates.

Weaknesses.label: Fixed Input/Output Lengths
Weaknesses.why: The `nn.Linear` layers map from a fixed `seq_len` to a fixed `pred_len`, meaning the model cannot natively handle variable input or output sequence lengths without architectural modifications or retraining.
Weaknesses.conditions: When flexibility in sequence lengths for inference or different prediction horizons is required without model re-initialization.

Resource Requirements & Complexity: The model has a relatively small number of parameters, which scales linearly with `seq_len`, `pred_len`, and `channels` (if `individual=True`, `2 * seq_len * pred_len * channels`; if `individual=False`, `2 * seq_len * pred_len`). Its computational complexity is dominated by the series decomposition (moving average filter, O(L*C)) and the linear layers (matrix multiplications, O(L_in * L_out * C)), making it highly efficient compared to attention-based models (which are typically O(L^2) or O(L log L)).
Paper Abstract: Recently, there has been a surge of Transformer-based solutions for the long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) task. Despite the growing performance over the past few years, we question the validity of this line of research in this work. Specifically, Transformers is arguably the most successful solution to extract the semantic correlations among the elements in a long sequence. However, in time series modeling, we are to extract the temporal relations in an ordered set of continuous points. While employing positional encoding and using tokens to embed sub-series in Transformers facilitate preserving some ordering information, the nature of the \emph{permutation-invariant} self-attention mechanism inevitably results in temporal information loss. To validate our claim, we introduce a set of embarrassingly simple one-layer linear models named LTSF-Linear for comparison. Experimental results on nine real-life datasets show that LTSF-Linear surprisingly outperforms existing sophisticated Transformer-based LTSF models in all cases, and often by a large margin. Moreover, we conduct comprehensive empirical studies to explore the impacts of various design elements of LTSF models on their temporal relation extraction capability. We hope this surprising finding opens up new research directions for the LTSF task. We also advocate revisiting the validity of Transformer-based solutions for other time series analysis tasks (e.g., anomaly detection) in the future. Code is available at: \url{https://github.com/cure-lab/LTSF-Linear}.

paper_claims.claim: Transformers are designed to extract semantic correlations in long sequences but may not be optimal for extracting temporal relations in ordered continuous time series data.
The permutation-invariant self-attention mechanism in Transformers inevitably leads to temporal information loss in time series modeling.
A simple one-layer linear model named LTSF-Linear outperforms existing sophisticated Transformer-based models for long-term time series forecasting across nine real-life datasets.
LTSF-Linear often outperforms Transformer-based models by a large margin in long-term time series forecasting tasks.
The paper conducts comprehensive empirical studies to explore how various design elements of LTSF models affect their temporal relation extraction capability.
The findings suggest revisiting the validity of Transformer-based solutions for other time series analysis tasks, such as anomaly detection.

论文摘要: Recently, there has been a surge of Transformer-based solutions for the long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) task. Despite the growing performance over the past few years, we question the validity of this line of research in this work. Specifically, Transformers is arguably the most successful solution to extract the semantic correlations among the elements in a long sequence. However, in time series modeling, we are to extract the temporal relations in an ordered set of continuous points. While employing positional encoding and using tokens to embed sub-series in Transformers facilitate preserving some ordering information, the nature of the \emph{permutation-invariant} self-attention mechanism inevitably results in temporal information loss. To validate our claim, we introduce a set of embarrassingly simple one-layer linear models named LTSF-Linear for comparison. Experimental results on nine real-life datasets show that LTSF-Linear surprisingly outperforms existing sophisticated Transformer-based LTSF models in all cases, and often by a large margin. Moreover, we conduct comprehensive empirical studies to explore the impacts of various design elements of LTSF models on their temporal relation extraction capability. We hope this surprising finding opens up new research directions for the LTSF task. We also advocate revisiting the validity of Transformer-based solutions for other time series analysis tasks (e.g., anomaly detection) in the future. Code is available at: \url{https://github.com/cure-lab/LTSF-Linear}.
